Private client visualization proposal

Not a render. A client approval experience.

I can create a 3D image from a 2D drawing — but for a studio producing high-value custom furniture and architectural metalwork, that is not the real opportunity.

The better opportunity is to help clients see the future piece clearly, emotionally, and confidently before fabrication begins.

Case scenarioBar-window cabinet / private client approval
RoleDirector-level visualization system
Strategic position

A technical drawing helps the shop build. A visualization system helps the client believe, approve, and value what is being built.

My recommendation is not to treat this as a simple 2D-to-3D task. That approach is too narrow for the quality of your work, the expectations of your clients, and the value of custom commissions.

My goal is to protect your time, support your client relationship, and give your team a repeatable way to turn early drawings into premium client-facing decisions.

1pilot project can prove the workflow
3approval layers: concept, refined, final
4closed black metal window panels
∞future reuse as standards/assets
Real case scenario

Bar-window cabinet: from technical request to premium presentation.

The client orders a bespoke cabinet / bar-window piece. The shop has technical drawings and fabrication logic, but the client needs to understand scale, proportion, finish, function, and mood.

Input: technical elevationNecessary for construction, but not optimized for luxury client understanding.
Input: metal-window geometryFour tall closed window panels, arched transom, tube proportions, hardware logic.
Output: refined approval imageA believable visual the client can react to before final fabrication detailing.
Output: 45° inspection viewShows depth, material, cabinet presence, and real spatial feeling.
Why the basic request is too small

A standalone 3D model does not solve the business problem.

Commodity render path

  • Receives a 2D drawing and produces one 3D-looking object.
  • Usually ignores client mood, environment, decision stage, and sales context.
  • May look attractive but still leaves the client unsure about finish, scale, usage, and approval.
  • Creates one deliverable, not a repeatable business advantage.

Director-led visualization path

  • Understands the studio, the client, the room, the material story, and the approval risk.
  • Creates early mockups, polished visuals, videos, finish options, and final approval assets.
  • Helps clients feel guided instead of overwhelmed by technical drawings.
  • Builds a repeatable visual system that can support future projects and marketing.
Video placeholders

Three simple animations to make the client feel the piece.

These are mobile placeholders for the three videos you created. Tap Attach video on your phone to preview each local file inside this proposal, or replace the placeholder paths in the HTML later.

01 — Human inspection passSlow left-to-right movement, as if a person is studying the cabinet.
▶
PurposeShows presence, proportion, reflection, and cabinet depth without changing the design.
02 — Detail and material passSlow movement across crown, fluted pilasters, glass, blackened steel, and stone.
▶
PurposeHelps the client appreciate craftsmanship, not just shape.
03 — Context / placement studyOptional outdoor or site-context test for scale, lighting, and staging.
▶
PurposeUseful when the client needs to understand how the object reads in a real environment.
Workflow analysis

The proposed client visualization system.

This is designed to support the company, not complicate it. The shop keeps its fabrication expertise. Lesly adds a client-facing visual layer that improves understanding, confidence, and approval.

1. Visual intake

Collect drawings, dimensions, room photos, finish direction, client goals, designer references, approval deadline, and risk areas.

2. Fast concept mockup

Create quick visual directions before heavy modeling. This helps the client say, “Yes, this is the right mood.”

3. Approval presentation

Produce polished mobile-friendly visuals: hero image, front view, 45° view, details, finish/mood board, and short videos.

4. Technical fidelity pass

After direction is approved, align proportions, hardware, materials, and key details with the refined shop drawings.

5. Optional 3D / interactive layer

Only build full 3D, animation, AR, or web viewer when the business case supports it.

6. Marketing conversion

Turn the project into “drawing to finished piece” content for website, Instagram, designer outreach, and future sales.

Compassion for the company

This is about protecting the client relationship.

I understand that custom fabrication is already complex. Your team has to manage client taste, designer expectations, engineering limits, materials, deadlines, and the reality of making a one-of-a-kind object.

My role is not to replace your drawings or your craft. My role is to create a visual bridge so your clients understand the work with more trust and less anxiety.

When the client sees the piece clearly earlier, the conversation becomes calmer. The team spends less time explaining. The designer has better material to present. The client feels personally guided.

Less confusion Faster approvals Better client care Higher perceived value Reusable marketing
Expertise map

Why Lesly fits this role.

She can interpret technical drawings, preserve proportions, build visual assets, and communicate object logic clearly.
The piece is not isolated. It must feel like it belongs in a home, wine room, bar, lodge, restaurant, or luxury interior.
Wood, blackened steel, bronze, glass, stone, patina, lighting, and reflections must support the client’s emotional decision.
Short videos and mobile presentation pages help clients review privately, share internally, and approve with confidence.
The bigger value is building a repeatable client-visualization workflow, not producing disconnected assets one by one.
Pay plan and scope

Recommended commercial structure.

These are indicative ranges for discussion, not a final quote. The right scope depends on number of visuals, accuracy requirements, revision rounds, video, and whether a reusable system is being built.

Entry pilot
$3.5k–$7.5k
One focused project: strategy note, 2–4 visuals, one simple video placeholder / direction.
Recommended
$8k–$18k
Client presentation package: concept, refined visuals, mood board, video sequence, questionnaire, and final approval assets.
Workflow buildout
$18k–$45k+
Reusable visualization system: templates, materials, asset library, intake workflow, and client presentation standards.
Fractional director
$6k–$15k/mo
Ongoing strategic visual leadership across multiple active client projects.
Fast estimate helper
Indicative starting point
$3,500
Client questionnaire

Questions before final scope.

1. Who is the visual presentation for?Owner, designer, architect, builder, private client, internal shop review, or sales presentation?
2. What decision needs to happen next?Approve shape, approve finish, approve open/closed function, approve budget, or simply understand the object?
3. What is the expected level of accuracy?Concept mood, close visual approximation, or dimensionally precise technical visualization?
4. What materials and finishes are being considered?Blackened steel, bronze, brass, stained wood, glass type, stone, patina, hardware finish.
5. What assets do you already have?PDF, DWG/DXF, CAD screenshots, sketches, shop notes, finish samples, site photos, reference interiors.
6. How will the client review it?Mobile page, PDF, email images, meeting presentation, private web page, video, or interactive model.
7. What would make this successful for your team?Fewer revisions, clearer approval, stronger sales presentation, better marketing, better designer communication.
Discuss pilot scope

The practical recommendation

Start with the bar-window cabinet as a focused pilot. Use it to prove that a client visualization system can turn technical drawings into a calmer, clearer, more premium approval experience.

Then decide together whether the best next move is a presentation package, workflow buildout, or ongoing fractional visualization leadership.
